{"status":"has_findings","summary":"[{\"summary\":\"Re-indexing is defined as a tag-wide delete, so one project refresh can wipe every code-intel record in the brain.\",\"evidence\":\"anti.md:119-121 recommends deleting by tag only: DELETE WHERE tags @> '{\\\"codeintel\\\"}', and clear_index(project_path) (anti.md:171) is not described as filtering by brain, project root, or index generation.\",\"why_it_matters\":\"If a brain holds more than one indexed repo, or a rebuild fails mid-run, this strategy destroys unrelated code graph data and leaves callers/callees queries incomplete with no recovery path.\",\"fix_instructions\":\"Add project-scoping metadata such as project_id/normalized root path and an index_batch_id to every code-intel symbol and edge. Make clear_index target only the current brain+project, or preferably build a new batch and atomically swap it active before deleting the previous batch.\",\"severity\":\"high\",\"file\":\"C:/Users/quangda/Downloads/nmem-hub-chr/anti.md\",\"line\":121,\"confidence\":\"high\"},{\"summary\":\"The plan never defines a stable symbol identifier, but every query API is name-based.\",\"evidence\":\"Neurons are modeled as '{kind}: {name} in {file}:{line}' with metadata limited to file/line/kind/language/signature (anti.md:102-105), while find_symbol/find_callers/find_callees and the MCP tools all take a plain name or symbol string (anti.md:172-175,198-201). The state machine stores edges before a later RESOLVING phase (anti.md:29-37), but no canonical target key is defined for duplicate or overloaded names.\",\"why_it_matters\":\"Common names repeated across files, classes, or imports will collapse into ambiguous graph nodes, producing wrong callers/callees and misleading impact analysis in production.\",\"fix_instructions\":\"Define a canonical symbol ID up front, for example project_id + language + module_path + qualified_name + signature + source range. Store unresolved references separately during parsing, then resolve edges to concrete neuron IDs after the full-project symbol table is built. Make the MCP APIs accept that ID or enough qualifiers to disambiguate.\",\"severity\":\"high\",\"file\":\"C:/Users/quangda/Downloads/nmem-hub-chr/anti.md\",\"line\":172,\"confidence\":\"high\"},{\"summary\":\"Phase 2 contradicts its own storage boundary by proposing raw asyncpg writes into core Neural Memory tables.\",\"evidence\":\"The storage phase says symbols will be stored 'qua Neural Memory API' (anti.md:161-165), but the implementation strategy is 'Dùng asyncpg trực tiếp để INSERT neurons/synapses' (anti.md:178-181). In the inspected wrapper, the supported integration point is PostgreSQLStorage via the plugin layer, not a documented raw-table contract (supabrain_plugin.py:26-79).\",\"why_it_matters\":\"Writing core tables directly couples this feature to internal schema details and can miss storage-layer invariants now or after upgrades, turning a library update into silent corruption, orphaned graph data, or failed startup migrations.\",\"fix_instructions\":\"Route writes through public PostgreSQLStorage/brain APIs if they can express the feature. If they cannot, put code-intel data in dedicated tables owned by this feature and query them from the MCP tool layer instead of inserting directly into shared Neural Memory tables.\",\"severity\":\"high\",\"file\":\"C:/Users/quangda/Downloads/nmem-hub-chr/anti.md\",\"line\":179,\"confidence\":\"high\"},{\"summary\":\"The indexing flow can expose a half-built graph because it writes live rows before resolution and defines no generation swap.\",\"evidence\":\"The state machine stores neurons and synapses during parsing, then performs RESOLVING only after all files are parsed (anti.md:29-37). clear_index(project_path) implies destructive rebuilds (anti.md:171), but there is no batch/version field, no stale-file pruning strategy, and the risk table omits interrupted-index handling (anti.md:288-295).\",\"why_it_matters\":\"A parser timeout, crash, or cancelled run leaves mixed old/new symbols and edges visible to production queries. Call graph and impact results then become internally inconsistent without any way for the client to detect that the index is incomplete.\",\"fix_instructions\":\"Introduce index generations with statuses such as building, active, and failed. Write all symbols and edges into a new generation, resolve references, validate counts, then atomically mark that generation active and garbage-collect the previous one. Expose generation status in the MCP API.\",\"severity\":\"high\",\"file\":\"C:/Users/quangda/Downloads/nmem-hub-chr/anti.md\",\"line\":29,\"confidence\":\"high\"},{\"summary\":\"Phase 1 does not actually implement most of the graph that the rest of the document promises.\",\"evidence\":\"The data model includes CALLS, IMPORTS, EXTENDS, IMPLEMENTS, and CONTAINS edges (anti.md:109-115), but the parser API only exposes scan_project, parse_file, and extract_calls (anti.md:145-147). The extraction matrix covers only a subset of constructs for four languages (anti.md:152-158) even though SUPPORTED advertises ten extensions (anti.md:139-143).\",\"why_it_matters\":\"If this ships as written, downstream tools will operate on a silently partial graph for most languages and relationship types. allFindings.summary: Re-indexing is defined as a tag-wide delete, so one project refresh can wipe every code-intel record in the brain.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Add project-scoping metadata such as project_id/normalized root path and an index_batch_id to every code-intel symbol and edge. Make clear_index target only the current brain+project, or preferably build a new batch and atomically swap it active before deleting the previous batch.
allFindings.why_it_matters: If a brain holds more than one indexed repo, or a rebuild fails mid-run, this strategy destroys unrelated code graph data and leaves callers/callees queries incomplete with no recovery path.

allFindings.summary: The plan never defines a stable symbol identifier, but every query API is name-based.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Define a canonical symbol ID up front, for example project_id + language + module_path + qualified_name + signature + source range. Store unresolved references separately during parsing, then resolve edges to concrete neuron IDs after the full-project symbol table is built. Make the MCP APIs accept that ID or enough qualifiers to disambiguate.
allFindings.why_it_matters: Common names repeated across files, classes, or imports will collapse into ambiguous graph nodes, producing wrong callers/callees and misleading impact analysis in production.

allFindings.summary: Phase 2 contradicts its own storage boundary by proposing raw asyncpg writes into core Neural Memory tables.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Route writes through public PostgreSQLStorage/brain APIs if they can express the feature. If they cannot, put code-intel data in dedicated tables owned by this feature and query them from the MCP tool layer instead of inserting directly into shared Neural Memory tables.
allFindings.why_it_matters: Writing core tables directly couples this feature to internal schema details and can miss storage-layer invariants now or after upgrades, turning a library update into silent corruption, orphaned graph data, or failed startup migrations.

allFindings.summary: The indexing flow can expose a half-built graph because it writes live rows before resolution and defines no generation swap.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Introduce index generations with statuses such as building, active, and failed. Write all symbols and edges into a new generation, resolve references, validate counts, then atomically mark that generation active and garbage-collect the previous one. Expose generation status in the MCP API.
allFindings.why_it_matters: A parser timeout, crash, or cancelled run leaves mixed old/new symbols and edges visible to production queries. Call graph and impact results then become internally inconsistent without any way for the client to detect that the index is incomplete.

allFindings.summary: Phase 1 does not actually implement most of the graph that the rest of the document promises.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Either narrow v1 to a clearly stated scope, such as JS/TS/Python function and import graph only, or add per-language extractor modules plus a capability matrix that disables unsupported edge types and languages at runtime.
allFindings.why_it_matters: If this ships as written, downstream tools will operate on a silently partial graph for most languages and relationship types. That is worse than an explicit unsupported-scope error because users will trust incorrect dependency results.

allFindings.summary: The new nmem_codeintel_index(path) tool is planned without the filesystem guardrails that the current wrapper already needs for built-in indexing.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Reuse the existing safe-path validation for the new tool and add explicit ignore rules for generated/vendor directories, maximum file size and file count budgets, symlink-cycle detection, and cancellation/timeouts surfaced in the tool response.
allFindings.why_it_matters: On a live MCP server, an unbounded path scan can walk node_modules, vendored trees, symlink loops, or sensitive directories, causing long hangs, resource spikes, or indexing the wrong codebase.

allFindings.evidence: The plan proposes monkey-patching in the style of _patch_path_validation(). However, _patch_path_validation() only patches the _index_scan and _train_docs methods of existing IndexHandler/TrainHandler classes — these are internal implementation methods. The MCP server's call_tool() uses an explicit dispatch dictionary mapping tool names to handler methods. Adding a new MCP tool endpoint requires adding an entry to this dispatch dict. There is no mechanism in supabrain_mcp.py to extend the dispatch table, and no precedent for it. The monkey-patch approach as described cannot expose new /tool handlers.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Before Phase 3, investigate neural_memory.mcp.server's tool registration mechanism. Options: (1) Subclass existing handlers, (2) Add tool definitions to the server's tool registry, (3) Contribute a CodeIntelHandler class to the upstream package, (4) Use a separate MCP server process. Document the chosen approach before coding Phase 3.

However, _patch_path_validation() only patches the _index_scan and _train_docs methods of existing IndexHandler/TrainHandler classes — these are internal implementation methods. The MCP server's call_tool() uses an explicit dispatch dictionary mapping tool names to handler methods. Adding a new MCP tool endpoint requires adding an entry to this dispatch dict. There is no mechanism in supabrain_mcp.py to extend the dispatch table, and no precedent for it. The monkey-patch approach as described cannot expose new /tool handlers.

However, _patch_path_validation() only patches the _index_scan and _train_docs methods of existing IndexHandler/TrainHandler classes — these are internal implementation methods. The MCP server's call_tool() uses an explicit dispatch dictionary mapping tool names to handler methods. Adding a new MCP tool endpoint requires adding an entry to this dispatch dict. There is no mechanism in supabrain_mcp.py to extend the dispatch table, and no precedent for it. The monkey-patch approach as described cannot expose new /tool handlers.
allFindings.suggested_fix: Investigate neural_memory/mcp/server.py's call_tool dispatch before Phase 3. Options: (1) Subclass the MCPServer and override call_tool to add codeintel dispatch entries, (2) Contribute a CodeIntelHandler mixin to the upstream package, (3) Use a separate MCP server process for codeintel tools, (4) Add codeintel tools as a plugin if the server supports plugin registration. Document the chosen approach explicitly in the plan before coding Phase 3.

allFindings.evidence: supabrain_mcp.py's _configure_postgres_backend() writes TOML config and sets env vars (NEURAL_MEMORY_POSTGRES_*). It does not import or expose any PostgreSQLStorage class. The actual PostgreSQLStorage class exists inside the neural_memory library at neural_memory/storage/postgres/postgres_store.py. Phase 2's CodeIntelStorage.__init__ signature is `def __init__(self, pg_storage: PostgreSQLStorage, brain_id: str)` — but since supabrain_mcp.py never instantiates or passes PostgreSQLStorage, CodeIntelStorage cannot be initialized as written. The plan assumes the class is accessible from the wrapper context.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Investigate the actual Neural Memory PostgreSQL storage API (import neural_memory.mcp.storage or similar). Determine whether asyncpg is used directly or through an ORM abstraction. Add a 'Phase 1.5: Storage API Discovery' step before Phase 2 begins. Document the actual class/method signatures.

allFindings.suggested_fix: Add a Phase 1.5 discovery step: investigate the actual PostgreSQLStorage API from neural_memory.storage.postgres.postgres_store. Determine how to obtain a storage instance in supabrain_mcp.py (e.g., via neural_memory.get_storage() or similar factory). Document the actual constructor signature and available methods before Phase 2 implementation begins.

allFindings.evidence: Duplicate of finding 7c91806a9ca59803. Same evidence: the plan declares clear_index(project_path: str) but the SQL is DELETE WHERE tags @> '{"codeintel"}' with no project_path filter. Confirmed by the same code-level contradiction.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Add project_path to the neuron metadata during storage. Modify clear_index to: DELETE FROM neurons WHERE tags @> '{"codeintel"' AND metadata->>'project_path' = $1. Ensure metadata includes project_path on every store_symbol call.

allFindings.evidence: The state machine for IMPACT analysis states: 'BFS graph traversal' with no depth limit mentioned. The MCP tools table shows nmem_codeintel_impact(symbol, depth?) with depth as optional. The plan's Risk table does not list unbounded BFS as a risk. In a codebase with 10K+ symbols and circular import chains, an unbounded BFS could traverse the entire graph, producing result sets that exceed MCP response limits or consume excessive memory.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Add hard limits: max_depth default=3, max_results default=100. Add circuit breaker for circular import detection (track visited neuron_ids in a set during BFS). Return a 'truncated: true' flag if results were cut.

allFindings.suggested_fix: Add hard limits: max_depth default=3, max_results default=100. Implement circular import detection using a visited set of neuron_ids during BFS traversal. Return a structured response: {symbols: [...], truncated: true/false, total_visited: N}. Consider a depth=1 'shallow impact' vs depth=3 'medium impact' vs depth=5 'deep impact' tiered API.

allFindings.evidence: The Mermaid diagram nests ParseFile, ExtractSymbols, ExtractEdges, StoreNeurons, StoreSynapses inside 'state PARSING { [*] --> ParseFile ...}'. In Mermaid stateDiagram-v2, a 'state NAME { ... }' block creates a submachine with its own initial state. The transitions 'StoreSynapses --> ParseFile : next file' and 'StoreSynapses --> [*] : all files done' originate from within this submachine. This correctly represents the file-by-file iteration, but the outer state transition 'PARSING --> RESOLVING' fires when the submachine reaches its final state, which is after all files are done. The diagram is technically valid Mermaid but the label 'PARSING' for the file-iteration submachine is misleading — 'PARSING_PER_FILE' would be clearer. The finding about 'concurrent substates' is a misinterpretation, but the diagram is indeed confusing.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Restructure the diagram: Show SCANNING iterating over file list, transitioning to PARSING once per file, then back to SCANNING for the next file. Or split into 'SCANNING → (loop over files) → PARSING_PER_FILE → RESOLVING → INDEXED'.

Add a comment noting this is a submachine invoked once per discovered file. Consider splitting into two diagrams: one for the top-level index lifecycle, one for the per-file processing loop.

allFindings.evidence: supabrain_mcp.py sets current_brain to a single env var value in config.toml. There is no brain-switching API in the wrapper. Phase 2's CodeIntelStorage takes brain_id as a parameter but supabrain_mcp.py never passes a per-project brain. The storage strategy does not describe using brain_id for isolation. The plan does not describe a multi-brain setup. The brain_id parameter creates an API contract that cannot be fulfilled given the single-brain wrapper design.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Either remove brain_id from the API (use single global brain with project_path in metadata for isolation), or implement multi-brain support in the wrapper before Phase 2. Document the chosen isolation strategy.

allFindings.suggested_fix: Remove brain_id parameter from CodeIntelStorage.__init__ since the wrapper uses a single global brain. Use project_path in metadata for multi-project isolation instead. Document that codeintel data is scoped to the configured brain with project_path metadata as the isolation key.

allFindings.evidence: The finding confirms that tree-sitter-languages is not installed ('pip show tree-sitter-languages returns exit code 1'). The plan adds tree-sitter>=0.23.0 and tree-sitter-languages>=1.10.0 to requirements.txt. On Windows, tree-sitter grammars bundle precompiled .node files that may have platform compatibility issues. Using >= instead of exact pins means a pip upgrade could silently break grammar parsing. The Risk table does not mention platform-specific grammar compatibility.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Pin exact versions: tree-sitter==0.23.2 tree-sitter-languages==1.10.2 (verify actual latest stable). Add a CI step to verify grammar parsing on Windows. Consider vendoring the specific grammars needed (JS, TS, Python) rather than the full bundle.

allFindings.suggested_fix: Pin exact verified versions: tree-sitter==0.23.2 tree-sitter-languages==1.10.2 (verify actual latest). Add a CI step that runs parser tests on Windows after installation. Add a lock file (requirements-lock.txt) or pip-compile output. Consider vendoring only the grammars needed (javascript, typescript, python) rather than the full bundle to reduce platform-specific surface area.

allFindings.evidence: The plan's data model maps codeintel symbols to neurons with field names 'neuron_type' (set to 'entity') and synapses with 'from_neuron_id'/'to_neuron_id'. The actual Neural Memory schema uses: neurons has 'type TEXT NOT NULL' (not 'neuron_type'), synapses has 'source_id' and 'target_id' (not 'from_neuron_id' and 'to_neuron_id'). Additionally, both neurons and synapses have 'brain_id TEXT NOT NULL' as the first component of their primary keys — the plan never mentions brain_id in its data model, which is a required field for all writes. The plan's schema assumptions are materially incorrect.
allFindings.fix_instructions: Add a 'Schema Verification' step: query the actual neurons and synapses tables to verify column names, types, and constraints before writing Phase 2 storage code. Compare pg_schema_ref.py against actual DB schema.

allFindings.suggested_fix: Before Phase 2, run a schema verification script that queries the actual neurons and synapses tables to confirm column names, types, constraints, and indexes. Update the data model section to match the actual schema: use 'type' instead of 'neuron_type', 'source_id'/'target_id' instead of 'from_neuron_id'/'to_neuron_id'. Include brain_id in all INSERT operations (extracted from the current storage context).

allFindings.evidence: The state machine diagram shows StoreNeurons --> StoreSynapses --> ParseFile (next file) with no transaction wrapper. Phase 2's storage strategy says 'dùng asyncpg trực tiếp' without mentioning transactions, rollback, or idempotency. The Risk table does not list partial-commit as a risk. If store_synapses fails mid-batch, the ERROR --> IDLE transition would leave some files' symbols already committed while others are not. A subsequent re-index would add duplicate neurons since there is no idempotency check (no unique constraint on content_hash for codeintel symbols).
allFindings.fix_instructions: Wrap each file's index in a transaction. On failure, rollback and log. Track indexing state (files indexed, last error) in a dedicated codeintel_index_state neuron so recovery is possible. Implement idempotency: check if symbol already exists (by content hash) before inserting.
allFindings.why_it_matters: The state machine diagram shows StoreNeurons --> StoreSynapses --> ParseFile (next file) with no transaction wrapper. Phase 2's storage strategy says 'dùng asyncpg trực tiếp' without mentioning transactions, rollback, or idempotency. The Risk table does not list partial-commit as a risk. If store_synapses fails mid-batch, the ERROR --> IDLE transition would leave some files' symbols already committed while others are not. A subsequent re-index would add duplicate neurons since there is no idempotency check (no unique constraint on content_hash for codeintel symbols).
allFindings.rationale: The state machine diagram shows StoreNeurons --> StoreSynapses --> ParseFile (next file) with no transaction wrapper. Phase 2's storage strategy says 'dùng asyncpg trực tiếp' without mentioning transactions, rollback, or idempotency. The Risk table does not list partial-commit as a risk. If store_synapses fails mid-batch, the ERROR --> IDLE transition would leave some files' symbols already committed while others are not. A subsequent re-index would add duplicate neurons since there is no idempotency check (no unique constraint on content_hash for codeintel symbols).
allFindings.suggested_fix: Wrap each file's index in an asyncpg transaction: BEGIN ... INSERT neurons ... INSERT synapses ... COMMIT. On failure: ROLLBACK and log to a codeintel_index_state neuron with file path and error. Add a unique constraint on neurons(brain_id, metadata->>'symbol_id') or a composite unique constraint on (content_hash, metadata->>'project_path') to enforce idempotency. Implement retry logic: if a file fails, mark it in the state neuron and retry on the next index call.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The plan's Phase 2 section shows the method signature as `async def clear_index(self, project_path: str)` — accepting a project_path parameter. However, the SQL it maps to is literally written as `DELETE WHERE tags @> '{"codeintel"}'` with no project_path filter. The File Structure section also shows `clear_index(project_path: str)`. This is a direct contradiction: a parameter is declared but never used in the SQL. Every codeintel neuron in the brain would be deleted on any re-index.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Add project_path to neuron metadata during store_symbol. Modify the SQL to: DELETE FROM neurons WHERE brain_id = $1 AND tags @> '{"codeintel"}' AND metadata->>'project_path' = $2. Store metadata={'project_path': ...} on every symbol store call.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The plan's data model defines no canonical symbol ID. All query APIs use name-based lookup: find_symbol(name: str), find_callers(symbol_name: str), find_callees(symbol_name: str). A common name like 'validate' or 'init' will exist across multiple files/languages/modules. The plan never defines a composite key (project + language + module_path + qualified_name + signature). This is an architectural gap that would produce wrong query results in production.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Define symbol_id = sha256(f"{project_path}:{language}:{module_path}:{qualified_name}:{signature}:{source_range}"). Store this as a dedicated field or in metadata->>'symbol_id'. Make all query APIs accept this ID or a disambiguation qualifier set (language + module_path + name + signature).

judgeVerdicts.rationale: Phase 2's storage strategy explicitly states: 'Dùng asyncpg trực tiếp (đã có trong project) để INSERT neurons/synapses'. This is raw asyncpg INSERT into the neurons and synapses tables, bypassing the PostgreSQLStorage class. The PostgreSQLStorage class (verified at neural_memory/storage/postgres/postgres_store.py) exists with proper CRUD methods through mixins (PostgresNeuronMixin, PostgresSynapseMixin). Writing directly with asyncpg can bypass any storage-layer invariants.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Use PostgreSQLStorage's neuron/synapse mixin methods instead of raw SQL. If the mixin API is insufficient for codeintel use cases, add feature-specific methods to PostgreSQLStorage, or use a dedicated codeintel_* table that doesn't share the neurons/synapses namespace.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The state machine diagram shows a linear pipeline: SCANNING → PARSING (loop ParseFile→ExtractSymbols→ExtractEdges→StoreNeurons→StoreSynapses) → RESOLVING → INDEXED. There is no concept of an 'index generation' or 'batch' with building/active/failed statuses. The plan describes no swap mechanism. If the process crashes between StoreNeurons and StoreSynapses, or during RESOLVING, a partial graph is left in the DB with no way to detect incompleteness.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Add an index_generations table with columns: id, project_path, status ('building'|'active'|'failed'), created_at. Store all symbols/edges with a generation_id instead of directly in neurons/synapses. After full resolution, verify counts and atomically update status='active'. Garbage-collect the previous generation. Expose generation status in the MCP response.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: Phase 1's deliverable is CodeIntelParser (~200 LOC) that parses files with tree-sitter and extracts symbols. The data model and all 5 MCP tools (search, callers, callees, impact, index) are described in later phases. The Phase 1 table only covers parser patterns (function_declaration, call_expression) for 4 languages. There is no per-language extractor module, no capability matrix, and no unsupported-feature error handling. The downstream tools would silently return empty results for unsupported languages/relationships.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Add a Phase 1.5 capability matrix step: define which languages and edge types (CALLS, IMPORTS, EXTENDS, etc.) are supported in v1. Add a runtime guard: if a file's language is unsupported, return a structured 'unsupported' response rather than silently skipping. Document this as explicit v1 scope.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The plan's Phase 3 adds nmem_codeintel_index(path) with input {path, extensions?, force?}. The existing _patch_path_validation() in supabrain_mcp.py provides safe-path validation for the existing index handler. The plan never mentions reusing this mechanism. There is no mention of ignore rules (node_modules, vendor), max file size, symlink-cycle detection, or timeout handling for the new tool. The plan's Risk table only covers 'tree-sitter crash on large files' with a 5s timeout, not filesystem traversal safety.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Add a safe_scan function that mirrors _safe() in _patch_path_validation: resolve paths, check against _BLOCKED list. Add ignore patterns (node_modules, .git, __pycache__, vendor). Add max_file_size=1MB, max_files=5000, symlink cycle detection, and timeout=300s with cancellation support. Surface these limits in the tool response metadata.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The plan proposes monkey-patching in the style of _patch_path_validation(). However, _patch_path_validation() only patches the _index_scan and _train_docs methods of existing IndexHandler/TrainHandler classes — these are internal implementation methods. The MCP server's call_tool() uses an explicit dispatch dictionary mapping tool names to handler methods. Adding a new MCP tool endpoint requires adding an entry to this dispatch dict. There is no mechanism in supabrain_mcp.py to extend the dispatch table, and no precedent for it. The monkey-patch approach as described cannot expose new /tool handlers.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Investigate neural_memory/mcp/server.py's call_tool dispatch before Phase 3. Options: (1) Subclass the MCPServer and override call_tool to add codeintel dispatch entries, (2) Contribute a CodeIntelHandler mixin to the upstream package, (3) Use a separate MCP server process for codeintel tools, (4) Add codeintel tools as a plugin if the server supports plugin registration. Document the chosen approach explicitly in the plan before coding Phase 3.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: supabrain_mcp.py's _configure_postgres_backend() writes TOML config and sets env vars (NEURAL_MEMORY_POSTGRES_*). It does not import or expose any PostgreSQLStorage class. The actual PostgreSQLStorage class exists inside the neural_memory library at neural_memory/storage/postgres/postgres_store.py. Phase 2's CodeIntelStorage.__init__ signature is `def __init__(self, pg_storage: PostgreSQLStorage, brain_id: str)` — but since supabrain_mcp.py never instantiates or passes PostgreSQLStorage, CodeIntelStorage cannot be initialized as written. The plan assumes the class is accessible from the wrapper context.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Add a Phase 1.5 discovery step: investigate the actual PostgreSQLStorage API from neural_memory.storage.postgres.postgres_store. Determine how to obtain a storage instance in supabrain_mcp.py (e.g., via neural_memory.get_storage() or similar factory). Document the actual constructor signature and available methods before Phase 2 implementation begins.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: Duplicate of finding 7c91806a9ca59803. Same evidence: the plan declares clear_index(project_path: str) but the SQL is DELETE WHERE tags @> '{"codeintel"}' with no project_path filter. Confirmed by the same code-level contradiction.

The state machine for IMPACT analysis states: 'BFS graph traversal' with no depth limit mentioned. The MCP tools table shows nmem_codeintel_impact(symbol, depth?) with depth as optional. The plan's Risk table does not list unbounded BFS as a risk. In a codebase with 10K+ symbols and circular import chains, an unbounded BFS could traverse the entire graph, producing result sets that exceed MCP response limits or consume excessive memory.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Add hard limits: max_depth default=3, max_results default=100. Implement circular import detection using a visited set of neuron_ids during BFS traversal. Return a structured response: {symbols: [...], truncated: true/false, total_visited: N}. Consider a depth=1 'shallow impact' vs depth=3 'medium impact' vs depth=5 'deep impact' tiered API.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The Mermaid diagram nests ParseFile, ExtractSymbols, ExtractEdges, StoreNeurons, StoreSynapses inside 'state PARSING { [*] --> ParseFile ...}'. In Mermaid stateDiagram-v2, a 'state NAME { ... }' block creates a submachine with its own initial state. The transitions 'StoreSynapses --> ParseFile : next file' and 'StoreSynapses --> [*] : all files done' originate from within this submachine. This correctly represents the file-by-file iteration, but the outer state transition 'PARSING --> RESOLVING' fires when the submachine reaches its final state, which is after all files are done. The diagram is technically valid Mermaid but the label 'PARSING' for the file-iteration submachine is misleading — 'PARSING_PER_FILE' would be clearer. The finding about 'concurrent substates' is a misinterpretation, but the diagram is indeed confusing.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Rename the submachine from 'PARSING' to 'PARSING_PER_FILE' to clarify that it's one iteration over a single file. Add a comment noting this is a submachine invoked once per discovered file. Consider splitting into two diagrams: one for the top-level index lifecycle, one for the per-file processing loop.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: supabrain_mcp.py sets current_brain to a single env var value in config.toml. There is no brain-switching API in the wrapper. Phase 2's CodeIntelStorage takes brain_id as a parameter but supabrain_mcp.py never passes a per-project brain. The storage strategy does not describe using brain_id for isolation. The plan does not describe a multi-brain setup. The brain_id parameter creates an API contract that cannot be fulfilled given the single-brain wrapper design.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Remove brain_id parameter from CodeIntelStorage.__init__ since the wrapper uses a single global brain. Use project_path in metadata for multi-project isolation instead. Document that codeintel data is scoped to the configured brain with project_path metadata as the isolation key.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The finding claims FTS is 'unspecified' and would require sequential ILIKE scans. However, the actual Neural Memory PostgreSQL schema (neural_memory/storage/postgres/postgres_schema.py) already includes: (1) content_tsv tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (to_tsvector('english', content)) STORED column on neurons, and (2) presumably a GIN index on that column (standard practice for pg_schema). The plan's FTS strategy using 'FTS match neurons' via the existing content_tsv column is therefore already supported by the schema. This is a false positive — the mechanism is specified by the existing infrastructure.

The finding claims a race hazard with concurrent index requests via os.chdir(). While os.chdir() is indeed a global process state change, the MCP protocol (stdio-based) is synchronous on the server side — neural_memory.mcp.server.main() runs in a single-threaded async event loop. Multiple concurrent index requests on the same MCP process would be serialized by the async event loop. The race condition described does not apply in the actual MCP server architecture. This finding is a speculative concern that doesn't match the runtime model.

The finding confirms that tree-sitter-languages is not installed ('pip show tree-sitter-languages returns exit code 1'). The plan adds tree-sitter>=0.23.0 and tree-sitter-languages>=1.10.0 to requirements.txt. On Windows, tree-sitter grammars bundle precompiled .node files that may have platform compatibility issues. Using >= instead of exact pins means a pip upgrade could silently break grammar parsing. The Risk table does not mention platform-specific grammar compatibility.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Pin exact verified versions: tree-sitter==0.23.2 tree-sitter-languages==1.10.2 (verify actual latest). Add a CI step that runs parser tests on Windows after installation. Add a lock file (requirements-lock.txt) or pip-compile output. Consider vendoring only the grammars needed (javascript, typescript, python) rather than the full bundle to reduce platform-specific surface area.

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The plan's data model maps codeintel symbols to neurons with field names 'neuron_type' (set to 'entity') and synapses with 'from_neuron_id'/'to_neuron_id'. The actual Neural Memory schema uses: neurons has 'type TEXT NOT NULL' (not 'neuron_type'), synapses has 'source_id' and 'target_id' (not 'from_neuron_id' and 'to_neuron_id'). Additionally, both neurons and synapses have 'brain_id TEXT NOT NULL' as the first component of their primary keys — the plan never mentions brain_id in its data model, which is a required field for all writes. The plan's schema assumptions are materially incorrect.
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Before Phase 2, run a schema verification script that queries the actual neurons and synapses tables to confirm column names, types, constraints, and indexes. Update the data model section to match the actual schema: use 'type' instead of 'neuron_type', 'source_id'/'target_id' instead of 'from_neuron_id'/'to_neuron_id'. Include brain_id in all INSERT operations (extracted from the current storage context).

judgeVerdicts.rationale: The state machine diagram shows StoreNeurons --> StoreSynapses --> ParseFile (next file) with no transaction wrapper. Phase 2's storage strategy says 'dùng asyncpg trực tiếp' without mentioning transactions, rollback, or idempotency. The Risk table does not list partial-commit as a risk. If store_synapses fails mid-batch, the ERROR --> IDLE transition would leave some files' symbols already committed while others are not. A subsequent re-index would add duplicate neurons since there is no idempotency check (no unique constraint on content_hash for codeintel symbols).
judgeVerdicts.suggested_fix: Wrap each file's index in an asyncpg transaction: BEGIN ... INSERT neurons ... INSERT synapses ... COMMIT. On failure: ROLLBACK and log to a codeintel_index_state neuron with file path and error. Add a unique constraint on neurons(brain_id, metadata->>'symbol_id') or a composite unique constraint on (content_hash, metadata->>'project_path') to enforce idempotency. Implement retry logic: if a file fails, mark it in the state neuron and retry on the next index call.
